home.title: Chemistry — Revision
home.intro: Eight core topics for G9 IB MYP Chemistry, matching the teacher's revision booklet. Each topic page has explanations and interactive simulations.

phIndicator.title: pH Indicator
phIndicator.description: Drag the pH slider; see colour change for universal, litmus and phenolphthalein. Real-world examples for each level.
phIndicator.scaleNote: Each pH unit = 10× change in [H⁺]. pH 1 has 10× more H⁺ than pH 2; 1000× more than pH 4.

examples.0: Battery acid, strong HCl — extremely corrosive.

atomBuilder.title: Atom Builder
atomBuilder.description: Pick element 1-20; see protons, neutrons, electrons and shell configuration on a Bohr model.
atomBuilder.shellNote: Shell capacities: 2, 8, 8, 18… Fill inner shells first.

moleCalc.title: Mole Calculator
moleCalc.description: Convert between mass, moles, and gas volume at STP. With buttons for common molar masses (H₂O, NaCl, CaCO₃...).
moleCalc.stpNote: STP = 0 °C, 100 kPa (IUPAC, 2019). 1 mole of any gas = 22.7 dm³. Particle count: 1 mol = 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro).

energyProfile.title: Energy Profile Diagram
energyProfile.description: Toggle exo/endo, adjust activation energy and ΔH — see the reaction-progress curve update.
energyProfile.exoSummary: products end up at lower energy than reactants. Heat is released. ΔH is negative.
energyProfile.endoSummary: products end up at higher energy than reactants. Heat is absorbed. ΔH is positive.
energyProfile.catalystNote: A catalyst would lower the peak (smaller E_a) without changing the start or end positions — ΔH stays the same.

atmosphere.title: The Atmosphere
atmosphere.intro: From the early atmosphere through to climate change. Topic 4 in the teacher's revision booklet.

history.title: History of the atmosphere
history.body: 4.6 bya — Earth formed. Early atmosphere: water vapour, CO₂, methane, ammonia (mainly from volcanoes). 3.5 bya — first photosynthetic bacteria release O₂. 2.4 bya — Great Oxygenation Event. Today: 78% N₂, 21% O₂, 0.9% argon, 0.04% CO₂, traces of others.

carbonRocks.title: Carbon locked in rocks
carbonRocks.body: Marine organisms used CO₂ to build shells (CaCO₃). When they died, shells settled and formed sedimentary rocks (limestone, chalk). This 'locked away' carbon for millions of years. Fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) similarly trap carbon from ancient plants and plankton.

greenhouse.title: Greenhouse effect
greenhouse.body: Sun's short-wave radiation passes through atmosphere → warms Earth's surface → Earth re-radiates as long-wave (infrared) → greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, H₂O, N₂O) absorb and re-emit it back → planet stays warm. Natural greenhouse keeps Earth at ~15°C. Without it: −18°C, no life.

items.cause: Human-enhanced greenhouse effect — burning fossil fuels and deforestation raise CO₂ from 280 ppm (pre-industrial) to 420+ ppm today.
items.evidence: Evidence: rising global temperatures, melting ice caps, sea level rise, ocean acidification, shifted seasons, more extreme weather.
items.consequences: Consequences: flooding of low-lying nations, droughts, biodiversity loss, food insecurity, climate refugees.
items.mitigation: Mitigation: renewables (solar, wind), carbon capture, reforestation, less meat, electric transport, energy efficiency.

cause.name: Causes of human-driven climate change
cause.rule: Burning fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) releases CO₂. Deforestation removes trees that would absorb CO₂. Agriculture (cattle, rice) releases methane (CH₄, 25× stronger than CO₂). Industrial processes release N₂O, fluorinated gases.
examples: Pre-industrial CO₂: 280 ppm. Today (2025): 425 ppm — 50% increase.
Top emitters: China (28%), USA (15%), EU (8%), India (7%), Russia (5%).
Per-capita: USA ~15 t/year, EU ~7, India ~2. Massive inequality.
Worst single contributor: coal-fired power plants. Cement production = 8% of emissions.
1.5°C target (Paris Agreement) — already +1.2°C above pre-industrial.
cause.tip: Greenhouse Gas Potential (GWP) measures relative warming power. CO₂ = 1 (baseline); methane = 28; N₂O = 273. Methane lasts ~12 years; CO₂ thousands.

evidence.name: Evidence climate is changing
evidence.rule: Multiple independent lines of evidence all point the same direction: temperature records, ice cores, sea level, glacier retreat, biodiversity shifts, extreme weather frequency.
examples: Direct temperature records since 1850: 1.2°C warmer, last 10 years all in top-10 hottest ever.
Ice cores (Vostok, EPICA): show CO₂ tracks temperature over 800,000 years.
Sea level: rising 3.4 mm/year now (was 1.5 mm/year in 1900s).
Arctic sea ice down 13% per decade.
Bird migration patterns shifted by weeks. Tropical diseases moving toward poles.
evidence.tip: Climate VARIABILITY (year-to-year) ≠ climate CHANGE (long-term trend). One cold winter doesn't disprove decades of warming.

consequences.name: Consequences
consequences.rule: Sea-level rise floods coastal cities. Crop failures from droughts and floods. Heatwaves cause excess deaths. Ocean acidification destroys coral reefs. Species migrations and extinctions. Climate refugees displace millions.
examples: Maldives, Tuvalu — entire countries at risk of being uninhabitable.
Australian bushfires 2019-20 killed 1 billion+ animals.
European heatwave 2003: 70,000 excess deaths.
Coral reefs: 50% lost since 1950 from warming + acidification.
By 2050, expected 200 million-1 billion climate refugees.
consequences.watchOut: Effects DISPROPORTIONATELY hurt poor countries that contributed LEAST to emissions. Climate justice is a moral issue, not just scientific.

mitigation.name: Mitigation — reducing emissions
mitigation.rule: Switching to renewable energy (solar, wind, hydro, nuclear). Energy efficiency (insulation, LED). Electrified transport. Reforestation. Carbon capture & storage. Carbon pricing (taxes, ETS). Plant-based diets.
examples: Costa Rica: 99% renewable electricity since 2015.
EU Emissions Trading Scheme: carbon trading, prices rose to €90+/ton.
Tesla and Chinese EVs: electric car sales now 18% of global new cars.
Methane cuts from cattle and rice can give near-instant temperature relief.
Direct Air Capture: Iceland Climeworks plant captures 4,000 t CO₂/year (still expensive).
mitigation.tip: Mitigation is about PREVENTING further damage. Adaptation is about COPING with damage already happening. Both needed.

adaptation.name: Adaptation — living with changing climate
adaptation.rule: Building sea walls and flood defences. Drought-resistant crops. Better forecasting and warning systems. Heatwave action plans. Air conditioning. Relocating communities. Insurance reform.
examples: Netherlands 'Room for the River' programme — controlled flooding zones.
Bangladesh: cyclone shelters reduced deaths from 500,000 (1970) to under 200 (recent storms).
Singapore: 2°C cooler street design, white roofs, urban trees.
Drought-resistant maize varieties in Sub-Saharan Africa.
London flood barrier (Thames Barrier) raised to handle higher sea levels.
adaptation.tip: Adaptation is expensive but unavoidable — even with full mitigation, the planet will warm further from existing CO₂. Some change is locked in.

items.co2: CO₂ — combustion of fossil fuels; greenhouse gas
items.co: CO (carbon monoxide) — incomplete combustion; toxic (binds to haemoglobin instead of O₂)
items.soot: Soot / particulates — incomplete combustion; respiratory disease, dirty buildings
items.sox: SO₂ (sulfur dioxide) — burning sulfur-containing fuels; acid rain (SO₂ + H₂O → H₂SO₃)
items.nox: NOx — high-temperature combustion (car engines); acid rain + photochemical smog

co2.name: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
co2.rule: Produced by COMPLETE combustion of any carbon fuel: C + O₂ → CO₂. Also from respiration. Main long-lived greenhouse gas. Concentration rising 2-3 ppm/year.
examples: Burning octane (petrol): 2C₈H₁₈ + 25O₂ → 16CO₂ + 18H₂O.
Average UK home emits 2-3 tonnes CO₂/year from heating.
Driving 1 mile in petrol car ≈ 400 g CO₂. EV = ~100 g (depending on grid).
Cement production releases CO₂ via decomposition: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ (chemical not combustion).
co2.tip: Not toxic at normal levels but dangerous at high concentrations (>5%) — can cause headaches, drowsiness, suffocation.

co.name: Carbon monoxide (CO)
co.rule: Produced by INCOMPLETE combustion (when O₂ supply is limited). 2C + O₂ → 2CO. Colourless, odourless — the 'silent killer'. Binds to haemoglobin 200× more tightly than O₂, preventing O₂ transport.
examples: Faulty boilers, blocked chimneys — UK kills ~50 people/year from CO poisoning.
Car exhaust in garage with door closed = lethal in minutes.
CO detectors (alarms) should be in every home with gas/oil heating.
Treatment: 100% O₂ for hours; severe cases hyperbaric chamber.
co.watchOut: Symptoms (headache, nausea, confusion) mimic flu — often unrecognised. Pets often collapse first; if so EVACUATE.

soot.name: Particulates (soot, PM2.5, PM10)
soot.rule: Tiny solid particles from incomplete combustion. PM10 (≤10 μm), PM2.5 (≤2.5 μm — finer, penetrates deep into lungs). Diesel engines, coal burning, wildfires.
examples: Beijing, Delhi: PM2.5 often 5-10× WHO limit. Population wears masks.
Diesel cars emit ~10× more particulates than petrol; banned in many cities.
Soot deposits dirty buildings — Big Ben blackened (cleaned 2017).
Wood-burning stoves: surprisingly polluting — UK now restricts in cities.
soot.tip: Linked to ~7 million premature deaths/year globally (WHO). Heart disease, stroke, lung cancer, asthma.

sox.name: Sulfur dioxide (SO₂)
sox.rule: Burning fuels containing sulfur impurities (coal especially): S + O₂ → SO₂. Reacts with water in atmosphere: SO₂ + H₂O → H₂SO₃ (sulfurous acid). Further oxidation → H₂SO₄ (sulfuric acid) = ACID RAIN.
examples: 1952 Great Smog of London — 12,000 deaths from coal SO₂ + fog.
Scandinavian forests damaged by acid rain from European coal in 1980s.
Modern coal plants use 'flue gas desulfurisation' — limestone scrubbers remove ~95% of SO₂.
Heavy fuel oil ships emit huge SO₂ at sea — new IMO limits 2020 cut levels 80%.
sox.tip: Acid rain effects: dissolves limestone buildings, leaches nutrients from soil, acidifies lakes, kills fish. Causes mainly historical now in West (regulations worked).

nox.name: Nitrogen oxides (NOx)
nox.rule: Formed at HIGH temperatures (car engines, power stations) by reaction of N₂ + O₂ in air: N₂ + O₂ → 2NO; further: 2NO + O₂ → 2NO₂. Causes acid rain AND photochemical smog.
examples: Diesel engines: highest NOx emitters. VW 'Dieselgate' 2015 — cheated on NOx tests; €30bn fines.
Photochemical smog (Los Angeles, Mexico City) — NOx + sunlight + VOCs → ozone (O₃) + irritants.
London: NO₂ above legal limit on many streets, especially near schools.
Catalytic converters convert NOx → N₂ + O₂ (but only when hot).
nox.watchOut: Ozone at GROUND level is harmful — irritates lungs, damages plants. Same molecule that protects us from UV in the stratosphere.

rates.title: Rates of Reaction
rates.intro: How fast a reaction goes — and the four factors that control it. Topic 6 in the teacher's revision booklet.

definition.title: What is rate?
definition.body: Rate = amount of product formed (or reactant used up) per unit time. Units: g/s, cm³/s, mol/dm³/s. Measured experimentally by tracking mass loss, gas volume produced, colour change, or precipitate cloudiness.

collision.title: Collision theory
collision.body: For a reaction to occur, particles must (1) collide and (2) collide with enough energy (≥ activation energy E_a) and (3) in the correct orientation. Anything that increases the frequency or energy of collisions speeds up the reaction.

temperature.name: Temperature
temperature.rule: Higher T → particles move FASTER and have MORE energy → more frequent AND more energetic collisions. A 10 °C rise roughly DOUBLES the rate.
examples: Food in the fridge lasts longer (low T slows bacterial growth).
Cooking pasta in boiling water (100 °C) is faster than in warm water (50 °C).
temperature.tip: Activation-energy effect dominates over speed effect. Drawing a Maxwell–Boltzmann curve: more particles cross E_a at higher T.

concentration.name: Concentration (and pressure for gases)
concentration.rule: More particles in the same volume → particles meet more often → more frequent collisions → faster rate. Doubling concentration usually doubles the rate (for simple reactions).
examples: Dilute HCl reacts slowly with Mg; concentrated HCl reacts quickly.
Increasing pressure on gases (e.g., Haber process) acts like concentration.
concentration.watchOut: Once reactants are dilute, the rate drops sharply — that's why curves flatten.

surfaceArea.name: Surface area (of solids)
surfaceArea.rule: More exposed surface → more places where collisions can happen → faster rate. Powder reacts much faster than a single lump of the same mass.
examples: Powdered marble + HCl fizzes vigorously; a marble lump reacts slowly.
Flour explosions in mills — fine powder gives huge surface area and combusts rapidly.
surfaceArea.tip: Total mass is the same, but surface area can be 100×+ greater for fine powder.

catalyst.name: Catalyst
catalyst.rule: Provides an ALTERNATIVE reaction pathway with a LOWER activation energy. The catalyst itself is NOT used up. Specific to a particular reaction.
examples: MnO₂ catalyses 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂ (rapid bubbling).
Iron in the Haber process (N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃).
Enzymes in your body — biological catalysts (amylase, pepsin).
catalyst.tip: Catalysts do NOT change ΔH or the products — only the speed.

graphs.title: Drawing rate graphs
graphs.body: Plot product (or reactant) vs time. Initial steep gradient = fast initial rate. Curve flattens as reactants run out. Steeper line = faster reaction. The gradient at any point = rate at that moment. Compare two reactions by comparing gradients of their tangents.

experiments.title: Carrying out a rate experiment
experiments.body: Classic example: marble chips + HCl. Measure mass loss (CO₂ escapes) over time with a balance. Independent variable: chip size / acid concentration / temperature. Dependent: mass at fixed time intervals. Control: same total marble mass, same acid volume, same temperature (unless studying T). Repeat 3 times, average.

extras.title: Chemistry — Extras
extras.intro: Topics that extend beyond the G9 syllabus — useful preview for IGCSE Extended and IB DP.

organic.title: Organic chemistry — intro
organic.body: Carbon forms 4 covalent bonds, allowing huge variety. Hydrocarbons = C + H only. Alkanes (CₙH₂ₙ₊₂) — single bonds, saturated, less reactive. Alkenes (CₙH₂ₙ) — at least one C=C double bond, more reactive (decolourise bromine water). Alcohols (R-OH) — react with sodium giving H₂. Carboxylic acids (R-COOH) — weak acids.

redox.title: Redox reactions
redox.body: OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons), Reduction Is Gain. Oxidation number: charge an atom would have if all bonds were ionic. Half-equations show electron transfer: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (oxidation). Reducing agents donate electrons. Common redox: rusting, combustion, batteries.

electrochem.title: Electrochemistry
electrochem.body: Electrolysis splits compounds using electricity. Cathode (−) attracts cations (metals, hydrogen). Anode (+) attracts anions (non-metals). Example: molten NaCl → Na at cathode, Cl₂ at anode. Used to extract reactive metals (Al, Na), electroplate, purify copper. Galvanic cells generate electricity from spontaneous redox.

rates.title: Rates of reaction
items: Concentration — more particles → more collisions → faster
Temperature — particles move faster, more energetic collisions
Surface area — more exposed reactant → faster (powder vs lump)
Catalyst — provides lower-energy pathway, not consumed
Light/pressure — for specific reactions (e.g., photosynthesis, gas phase)

equilibrium.title: Dynamic equilibrium — intro
equilibrium.body: Reversible reactions reach a state where forward and reverse rates are equal, but reactions continue. Le Chatelier's principle: if disturbed (T, p, conc.), the equilibrium shifts to oppose the change. Example: Haber process N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ — high pressure + moderate T + catalyst (iron) maximises ammonia yield.

atomsPeriodic.title: Atomic Structure & Periodic Table
atomsPeriodic.intro: Atoms, isotopes, electronic configuration, and how the periodic table organises elements.

atomicStructure.title: Atomic structure
atomicStructure.body: Atoms have a tiny nucleus (protons + neutrons) and orbiting electrons. Protons are positive (+), neutrons neutral (0), electrons negative (−). Atomic number Z = number of protons (defines element). Mass number A = protons + neutrons.

isotopes.title: Isotopes
isotopes.body: Same element, different number of neutrons. Same Z, different A. Identical chemical behaviour, slightly different physical (mass-dependent). Example: ¹²C and ¹⁴C.

electronConfig.title: Electronic configuration
electronConfig.body: Electron shells fill in order: 2, 8, 8, 18… Inner shells first. Example: sodium (Z=11) → 2,8,1. The outer shell (valence) determines reactivity.

periodicTable.title: Reading the periodic table
periodicTable.body: Groups (columns) share outer electrons and chemistry. Periods (rows) = number of shells. Group 1 = alkali metals (reactive). Group 7 = halogens. Group 0/8 = noble gases (inert, full outer shell).

trends.title: Periodic trends
trends.body: Down Group 1: more reactive (outer electron further from nucleus, easier to lose). Down Group 7: less reactive. Across a period: metallic → non-metallic character.

bonding.title: Bonding
bonding.intro: How atoms join: ionic, covalent, metallic. Each gives different properties.

ionic.title: Ionic bonding
ionic.body: Metal + non-metal. Metal LOSES electrons (forms cation, +). Non-metal GAINS electrons (anion, −). Opposite charges attract. Example: Na⁺ + Cl⁻ → NaCl. High melting point, conducts when molten or dissolved, crystalline.

covalent.title: Covalent bonding
covalent.body: Non-metal + non-metal. Atoms SHARE electrons to fill outer shell. Single bond = 1 pair shared, double = 2 pairs (O=O), triple = 3 (N≡N). Simple molecules: low melting point, don't conduct. Giant covalent (diamond, graphite): very high melting.

metallic.title: Metallic bonding
metallic.body: Sea of delocalised electrons surrounding positive metal ions. Explains: conducts (mobile electrons), malleable (layers slide), high melting point (strong attraction), shiny (free electrons reflect light).

items.ionic: Ionic: high mp, brittle, conducts when molten/aq
items.simpleCovalent: Simple covalent (H₂O, CO₂): low mp, non-conductor
items.giantCovalent: Giant covalent (diamond, SiO₂): very high mp, hard, non-conductor
items.metallic: Metallic: high mp, malleable, conductor, lustrous

ionic.name: Ionic structures
ionic.rule: Giant lattice of alternating + and − ions held by strong electrostatic forces. HIGH melting point (lots of energy to break). BRITTLE (force shifts ions, like-like meet, repel and shatter). Conducts ONLY when molten or dissolved (ions free to move).
examples: NaCl (table salt): mp 801 °C. Dissolves in water → conducts.
MgO: mp 2852 °C (Mg²⁺ stronger than Na⁺).
CaF₂ (fluorite): used in optical lenses.
Solid NaCl does NOT conduct (ions locked in place). Molten NaCl DOES.
ionic.tip: Higher charge = stronger bonds = higher mp. Mg²⁺O²⁻ has DOUBLE charge each → much higher mp than Na⁺Cl⁻.

simpleCovalent.name: Simple molecular (covalent)
simpleCovalent.rule: Small molecules with strong covalent bonds INSIDE but weak intermolecular forces BETWEEN. LOW mp/bp (only need to break weak forces). Do NOT conduct (no charged particles).
examples: H₂O: mp 0 °C, bp 100 °C. Liquid at room temp.
CO₂: bp −78 °C (sublimes to gas).
I₂: solid at room temp but melts at 114 °C — bigger molecules = stronger intermolecular forces.
O₂, N₂: gases at room temp.
simpleCovalent.tip: Bigger molecules = stronger London forces = higher mp/bp. Compare F₂ (gas) < Cl₂ (gas) < Br₂ (liquid) < I₂ (solid).

giantCovalent.name: Giant covalent (macromolecular)
giantCovalent.rule: Network of atoms held by STRONG covalent bonds throughout. EXTREMELY HIGH mp (must break MANY strong bonds). Very HARD. Usually do NOT conduct (exception: graphite).
examples: Diamond: mp ~4000 °C. Every C bonded to 4 others tetrahedrally. Hardest natural substance.
Graphite: also pure C but arranged in layers. Layers slide → soft + lubricant. Free electrons → CONDUCTS.
SiO₂ (quartz): mp 1713 °C. Sand, glass-making.
Silicon: foundation of microchips. Used as semiconductor.
giantCovalent.watchOut: Diamond and graphite are ALLOTROPES of carbon — same element, different structures, very different properties.

metallic.name: Metallic bonding
metallic.rule: Cations arranged in lattice, surrounded by 'sea' of delocalised electrons. Strong attraction → high mp. Free electrons → CONDUCTS heat and electricity. Layers slide easily → MALLEABLE and DUCTILE. Lustrous.
examples: Iron: mp 1538 °C. Strong but rusts (oxidises).
Copper: best conductor of electricity (after silver). Used in wiring.
Gold: doesn't oxidise → jewellery, electronics contacts.
Aluminium: low density + corrosion-resistant → aeroplanes, drinks cans.
Alloys (brass, steel) — different sizes disrupt sliding → harder.
metallic.tip: All four properties (conducts, malleable, high mp, lustrous) come from the SAME thing: the sea of delocalised electrons. Mention this in answers.

reactions.title: Reactions & Equations
reactions.intro: Types of chemical reactions and how to balance equations correctly.

combination.name: Combination (synthesis)
combination.rule: Two or more substances combine to form ONE product. General form: A + B → AB.
examples: 2H₂(g) + O₂(g) → 2H₂O(l) — hydrogen burns in oxygen
CaO(s) + H₂O(l) → Ca(OH)₂(aq) — quicklime + water
N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) → 2NH₃(g) — Haber process
combination.tip: Almost always exothermic — bond formation releases energy.

decomposition.name: Decomposition
decomposition.rule: ONE compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances. Often needs heat, light, or electricity. General: AB → A + B.
examples: 2H₂O₂(aq) → 2H₂O(l) + O₂(g) — peroxide with MnO₂ catalyst
CaCO₃(s) → CaO(s) + CO₂(g) — limestone heated
2H₂O(l) → 2H₂(g) + O₂(g) — electrolysis of water
decomposition.tip: Thermal decomposition of carbonates is a classic G9 example.

displacement.name: Single displacement
displacement.rule: A more reactive metal displaces a less reactive metal from its compound. Driven by the reactivity series (K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Cu > Ag).
examples: Zn(s) + CuSO₄(aq) → ZnSO₄(aq) + Cu(s) — blue colour fades, brown copper deposits
Mg(s) + 2HCl(aq) → MgCl₂(aq) + H₂(g) — magnesium 'fizzes'
displacement.watchOut: If the metal you're adding is LESS reactive than the one in the compound, NO reaction happens (Cu added to ZnSO₄ → nothing).

doubleDisplacement.name: Double displacement (precipitation)
doubleDisplacement.rule: Two soluble compounds swap partners. If one product is INSOLUBLE, you see a precipitate. AB + CD → AD + CB.
examples: AgNO₃(aq) + NaCl(aq) → AgCl(s)↓ + NaNO₃(aq) — white precipitate of silver chloride
Pb(NO₃)₂(aq) + 2KI(aq) → PbI₂(s)↓ + 2KNO₃(aq) — bright yellow precipitate
doubleDisplacement.tip: Memorise common insoluble compounds: AgCl, BaSO₄, CaCO₃, PbI₂. They appear as the precipitate.

combustion.name: Combustion
combustion.rule: Substance reacts rapidly with oxygen, releasing heat and light. Hydrocarbon + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O. Complete combustion → CO₂; incomplete (not enough O₂) → toxic CO and soot.
examples: CH₄(g) + 2O₂(g) → CO₂(g) + 2H₂O(g) — methane gas
2C₈H₁₈(l) + 25O₂(g) → 16CO₂(g) + 18H₂O(g) — petrol (octane)
combustion.watchOut: Incomplete combustion (yellow flame, soot) is a hazard — CO is odourless and toxic. Always ventilate.

neutralisation.name: Neutralisation
neutralisation.rule: Acid + base → salt + water. The H⁺ from the acid combines with OH⁻ from the alkali. Always slightly exothermic.
examples: HCl(aq) + NaOH(aq) → NaCl(aq) + H₂O(l)
H₂SO₄(aq) + 2KOH(aq) → K₂SO₄(aq) + 2H₂O(l)
2HNO₃(aq) + Mg(OH)₂(s) → Mg(NO₃)₂(aq) + 2H₂O(l)
neutralisation.tip: The ionic equation is always the same: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O. Useful shortcut.

balancing.title: Balancing equations — step by step
balancing.body: 1) Write word equation. 2) Convert to formulae. 3) Count atoms on each side. 4) Add coefficients (NOT subscripts) to balance. 5) Check H and O last. Example: __ Fe + __ O₂ → __ Fe₂O₃ → 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃.

stateSymbols.title: State symbols
stateSymbols.body: (s) solid, (l) liquid, (g) gas, (aq) aqueous (dissolved in water). Always include in answers.

ionic.title: Ionic equations
ionic.body: Write only the ions that actually react. Spectator ions are omitted. Example: H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l) for any strong acid + strong alkali.

stoichiometry.title: Stoichiometry
stoichiometry.intro: Quantitative chemistry — how much of each substance reacts.

moles.title: The mole
moles.body: 1 mole = 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro). n = m / M, where m is mass (g) and M is molar mass (g/mol). At STP (0 °C, 100 kPa), 1 mol gas = 22.7 dm³.

molarMass.title: Molar mass
molarMass.body: Sum of atomic masses from the periodic table. Example: H₂O = 2(1) + 16 = 18 g/mol. CaCO₃ = 40 + 12 + 3(16) = 100 g/mol.

calculations.title: Reaction calculations
calculations.body: 1) Balanced equation. 2) Find moles of given. 3) Use mole ratio from equation. 4) Convert moles to mass/volume. Example: 4g of Mg burns in O₂ — how much MgO? n(Mg) = 4/24 = 0.167 mol → 0.167 mol MgO → mass = 0.167 × 40 = 6.67 g.

limiting.title: Limiting reactant
limiting.body: The reactant fully used up first. Determines product amount. Calculate moles of each, divide by coefficient — smallest result is the limiting one.

percentYield.title: Percent yield
percentYield.body: % yield = (actual mass ÷ theoretical mass) × 100. Theoretical is from stoichiometry assuming complete reaction.

acidsBases.title: Acids, Bases & Salts
acidsBases.intro: pH scale, indicators, and key reactions of acids.

acid.name: Acid
acid.rule: A substance that releases H⁺ (hydrogen) ions in water. Sour taste, turns litmus red, pH < 7.
examples: HCl (hydrochloric acid) — stomach acid
H₂SO₄ (sulfuric acid) — car batteries
HNO₃ (nitric acid) — fertilisers
CH₃COOH (ethanoic / acetic acid) — vinegar (weak)
acid.tip: Strong acids fully ionise in water (HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻). Weak acids only partly ionise (CH₃COOH ⇌ CH₃COO⁻ + H⁺).

base.name: Base
base.rule: A substance that accepts H⁺ ions. Reacts with acids to form salt + water. Often metal oxides, hydroxides or carbonates.
examples: CuO (copper oxide) — black solid that reacts with acid to give blue solution
MgO (magnesium oxide) — white powder
CaCO₃ (calcium carbonate) — chalk, limestone
base.tip: Not all bases dissolve in water. Bases that DO dissolve are called alkalis.

alkali.name: Alkali
alkali.rule: A soluble base — it dissolves in water and releases OH⁻ (hydroxide) ions. Bitter taste, slippery feel, turns litmus blue, pH > 7.
examples: NaOH (sodium hydroxide) — drain cleaner
KOH (potassium hydroxide) — soaps
Ca(OH)₂ (calcium hydroxide, limewater) — used to test for CO₂
alkali.watchOut: Strong alkalis (NaOH, KOH) are extremely corrosive — wear goggles.

salt.name: Salt
salt.rule: An ionic compound formed when an acid is neutralised. The H⁺ of the acid is replaced by a metal ion (or NH₄⁺).
examples: HCl + NaOH → NaCl (table salt) + H₂O
H₂SO₄ + Cu(OH)₂ → CuSO₄ (blue) + 2H₂O
HNO₃ + KOH → KNO₃ + H₂O
salt.tip: Name a salt: first word from the metal/base, second from the acid. HCl → chloride; H₂SO₄ → sulfate; HNO₃ → nitrate.

pH.title: pH scale
pH.body: 0–7 acidic, 7 neutral, 7–14 alkaline. Each pH unit = 10× change in [H⁺]. Strong acid pH ≈ 0–2 (e.g., battery acid, stomach acid). Weak acid pH ≈ 3–6 (vinegar, lemon juice). Pure water pH = 7 (neutral). Weak alkali pH ≈ 8–10 (sea water, baking soda). Strong alkali pH ≈ 11–14 (ammonia, drain cleaner).

litmus.name: Litmus
litmus.rule: A natural dye from lichen. RED in acid, BLUE in alkali. Simple — only tells you 'acid or alkali', not the exact pH.
litmus.tip: Mnemonic: 'Litmus turns blue when it's basic'. Used as litmus paper (cheaper) or solution.

phenolphthalein.name: Phenolphthalein
phenolphthalein.rule: Colourless in acid AND neutral. Pink in alkali (pH > 8.2). Most common indicator for acid–alkali titrations.
phenolphthalein.tip: End point: change from colourless to first faint pink. Stop adding acid as soon as you see the pink fade.

universal.name: Universal indicator
universal.rule: A MIX of several indicators. Gives a full spectrum: red (pH 0) → orange → yellow → green (pH 7) → blue → purple (pH 14).
universal.tip: Useful for ESTIMATING pH to ±1 unit. For precise readings, use a pH meter or probe.

methylOrange.name: Methyl orange (bonus)
methylOrange.rule: RED in acid (pH < 3.1), YELLOW in alkali (pH > 4.4). Used for strong acid + weak alkali titrations.
methylOrange.tip: Pairs with phenolphthalein for strong alkali + weak acid titrations.

reactions.title: Acid reactions — the FOUR you must memorise

metal.name: Acid + reactive metal → salt + hydrogen
metal.rule: Only metals ABOVE hydrogen in the reactivity series react (Mg, Al, Zn, Fe — yes; Cu, Ag, Au — no). Bubbles of H₂ form; test with a lit splint → 'squeaky pop'.
examples: Zn(s) + 2HCl(aq) → ZnCl₂(aq) + H₂(g)
Mg(s) + H₂SO₄(aq) → MgSO₄(aq) + H₂(g)
2Al(s) + 6HCl(aq) → 2AlCl₃(aq) + 3H₂(g)
metal.watchOut: Reactive group 1 metals (Na, K) react EXPLOSIVELY with acid — never test these in school.

base.name: Acid + base → salt + water (no gas)
base.rule: Metal oxides and hydroxides neutralise acids. No gas given off (unlike with carbonates). Often used to make pure salts.
examples: 2HCl(aq) + CuO(s) → CuCl₂(aq) + H₂O(l) — black solid turns blue solution
H₂SO₄(aq) + ZnO(s) → ZnSO₄(aq) + H₂O(l)
2HNO₃(aq) + Mg(OH)₂(s) → Mg(NO₃)₂(aq) + 2H₂O(l)
base.tip: Heat acid in beaker → add base until no more dissolves → filter → evaporate → crystallise. Classic 'making a salt' lab.

carbonate.name: Acid + carbonate → salt + water + CO₂
carbonate.rule: Carbonates (CaCO₃, Na₂CO₃, CuCO₃) fizz when acid is added. Test the gas with limewater — turns CLOUDY → confirms CO₂.
examples: 2HCl(aq) + CaCO₃(s) → CaCl₂(aq) + H₂O(l) + CO₂(g) — marble chips classic
H₂SO₄(aq) + Na₂CO₃(aq) → Na₂SO₄(aq) + H₂O(l) + CO₂(g)
carbonate.tip: Acid rain attacks limestone buildings via this reaction — over decades, statues are eroded.

alkali.name: Acid + alkali → salt + water (neutralisation)
alkali.rule: The 'pure' neutralisation reaction. Ionic equation always: H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l). Used in titrations.
examples: HCl(aq) + NaOH(aq) → NaCl(aq) + H₂O(l)
H₂SO₄(aq) + 2NaOH(aq) → Na₂SO₄(aq) + 2H₂O(l)
HNO₃(aq) + KOH(aq) → KNO₃(aq) + H₂O(l)
alkali.tip: Slight temperature rise — neutralisation is exothermic (~57 kJ/mol of water formed).

energy.title: Energy Changes
energy.intro: Exothermic and endothermic reactions, energy diagrams, calculations.

items.exothermic: Exothermic — releases heat (surroundings warm up). ΔH negative. e.g., combustion, neutralisation, most reactions.
items.endothermic: Endothermic — absorbs heat (surroundings cool). ΔH positive. e.g., thermal decomposition, dissolving NH₄NO₃.

exothermic.name: Exothermic reactions
exothermic.rule: Energy is RELEASED to the surroundings (which get warmer). The products have LESS energy than reactants. ΔH is NEGATIVE. Energy difference comes from forming new bonds (releases more energy than breaking old bonds).
examples: Combustion: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O, ΔH = −890 kJ/mol.
Neutralisation: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O, ΔH = −57 kJ/mol.
Respiration in your body: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ENERGY (powers life).
Hand warmers — iron oxidation: 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃ + heat. Activated by air.
Self-heating cans of coffee.
exothermic.tip: On an energy profile diagram: products LOWER than reactants. ΔH = E(products) − E(reactants) < 0.

endothermic.name: Endothermic reactions
endothermic.rule: Energy is ABSORBED from the surroundings (which get colder). Products have MORE energy than reactants. ΔH is POSITIVE. Breaking bonds takes more energy than is released forming new ones.
examples: Thermal decomposition: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂, ΔH = +178 kJ/mol (needs strong heating).
Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + LIGHT → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ (sun provides energy).
Dissolving ammonium nitrate: NH₄NO₃(s) + H₂O → NH₄⁺(aq) + NO₃⁻(aq) — solution gets COLD.
Cold packs in first aid kits — break sachet, NH₄NO₃ dissolves, ice-cold instantly.
Sherbet sweets: sodium bicarbonate + citric acid = cold tingle on tongue.
endothermic.watchOut: Endothermic ≠ unusual. Many natural processes (cooking, photosynthesis) are endothermic. Don't assume reactions release energy.

profiles.title: Energy profile diagrams
profiles.body: x-axis: reaction progress. y-axis: energy. Reactants → activation energy peak → products. Exothermic: products lower than reactants. Endothermic: products higher. Catalyst lowers activation energy without changing ΔH.

bondEnergies.title: Bond energies
bondEnergies.body: Breaking bonds requires energy (endothermic). Making bonds releases energy (exothermic). ΔH = (energy in to break bonds) − (energy out from new bonds). If +ve: endothermic; if −ve: exothermic.

calorimetry.title: Calorimetry
calorimetry.body: q = m × c × ΔT (heat in joules = mass × specific heat capacity × temperature change). For water c = 4.18 J/g·°C. Then ΔH (in kJ/mol) = q / (1000 × n).
